question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user flood GET /health/readiness with high-concurrency requests to exhaust the PostgreSQL connection pool (via the 'postgresql' readiness check), causing the node to report ServiceUnavailable and be removed from load-balancer rotation, thereby preventing all transaction gossip submissions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a nil-pointer panic by racing the /health/readiness endpoint at a moment when /network/list returns an empty NetworkIdentifiers slice, causing networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] to panic and crash the readiness handler, marking the node as not ready and blocking transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call GET /health/readiness with no rate limiting in place to cause the 'postgresql' health check to open a new DB connection on every invocation, exhausting the database connection pool and causing the node to fail readiness, preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user send thousands of concurrent GET /health/readiness requests to force repeated internal calls to http://localhost:{port}/network/list and /network/status, saturating the local HTTP server's goroutine pool and causing legitimate transaction-submit requests to be dropped or delayed, preventing gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of authentication on the GET /health/readiness endpoint to repeatedly trigger the 10-second-timeout PostgreSQL check, holding open DB connections for up to 10 seconds each, exhausting the pool and starving the construction/submit path of DB access needed for transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the readiness check to permanently fail by sending a crafted response to /network/list that returns an empty NetworkIdentifiers array, triggering an index-out-of-bounds panic at networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0], crashing the readiness goroutine and marking the node unready, blocking all transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user abuse the unauthenticated GET /health/liveness endpoint to enumerate that the liveness check has no registered checks (always returns OK), then pivot to flooding /health/readiness to cause a readiness failure without triggering a liveness-based pod restart, creating a persistent state where the node is alive but not routing transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() reuses a single rosettaClient instance across all readiness check invocations to cause a race condition under concurrent /health/readiness requests, corrupting shared client state and causing the network check to permanently return errors, blocking transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of rate limiting on GET /health/readiness to generate a sustained flood that causes the hellofresh/health-go library to queue check goroutines faster than they complete, leading to goroutine exhaustion and a full service crash that prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 10-second timeout on the 'network' readiness check by sending slow-loris-style requests to the /network/list endpoint, holding the check open for the full timeout duration on every /health/readiness poll, causing Kubernetes to observe repeated 503s and remove the pod from service, preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SkipOnErr: false configuration on both readiness checks to ensure that any transient error in either the 'postgresql' or 'network' check causes the entire readiness endpoint to return 503, and by repeatedly triggering such errors via flooding, maintain a persistent not-ready state that prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user cause information disclosure by reading the JSON body of a failed GET /health/readiness response, which may include the PostgreSQL DSN (host, port, username, dbname) embedded in error details from the postgres.New() check, enabling further targeted attacks to prevent transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that /health/liveness and /health/readiness are listed in internalPaths (trace.go) causing their access to be logged only at DEBUG level, meaning a sustained flood of readiness requests goes undetected in production logs, allowing the attacker to silently maintain a not-ready state and prevent transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential nature of checkNetworkStatus() (first /network/list, then /network/status) to cause a time-of-check/time-of-use race where the network list succeeds but the network status call fails due to a concurrent state change, causing the readiness check to fail and preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the PostgreSQL readiness check uses a separate connection (via postgres.New()) rather than the application's existing connection pool, to cause the database to hit its max_connections limit by flooding /health/readiness, starving the main application of DB connections needed for transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of request body size limits on GET /health/readiness to send oversized HTTP headers that cause the health handler to consume excessive memory per request, leading to OOM and service crash that prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the unauthenticated /health/readiness endpoint to perform a timing side-channel attack, measuring response latency to infer whether the PostgreSQL or network check is failing, and use this information to time targeted attacks that prevent transaction gossip at critical moments?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a denial-of-service by sending HTTP/1.1 keep-alive connections to /health/readiness that hold the connection open after the response, consuming file descriptors and preventing new connections from being accepted, ultimately blocking transaction gossip submissions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that both readiness checks run concurrently with a shared 10-second timeout to cause a thundering-herd effect where many simultaneous /health/readiness requests each spawn two goroutines (postgresql + network checks), exhausting the goroutine scheduler and causing transaction gossip requests to be starved?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus() constructs the server URL as http://localhost:{port} (plaintext HTTP) to perform a man-in-the-middle attack on the loopback interface in a shared-network container environment, injecting a crafted /network/list response with an empty NetworkIdentifiers array to trigger a panic and prevent transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of CORS restrictions or IP allowlisting on /health/readiness to call it from a browser-based SSRF vector, leaking the health check JSON response body that reveals internal service state and database connectivity details useful for planning a transaction gossip disruption attack?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the rosettaClient in checkNetworkStatus() is created with client.NewConfiguration() using no TLS (http://localhost) and no authentication, so that in a compromised container network, the attacker can intercept the internal /network/list call and return a response that causes the readiness check to fail, preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the health controller is registered in both online and offline router modes (main.go lines 105, 143) to call /health/readiness on the offline node and observe that the network check always fails (offline mode has no NetworkStatus), leaking the operational mode of the node and enabling targeted attacks to prevent transaction gossip on the online node?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a circuit breaker on the readiness check to cause a cascading failure where repeated /health/readiness calls each attempt a new PostgreSQL connection that times out after 10 seconds, creating a backlog of pending connections that eventually causes the database server to reject all new connections, preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that /health/readiness returns a detailed JSON body (health.Check struct) including the names and statuses of individual checks ('postgresql', 'network') to fingerprint the exact health check configuration and craft targeted attacks against each dependency to prevent transaction gossip?
